---
title: "IL NPA — distillation of ask and argument"
subtitle: "Editable input for prefiled-testimony skeleton prompt"
author:
  - name: Juan-Pablo Velez
    email: jpv@switch.box
    affiliations:
      - Switchbox
date: 2026-04-29
toc: true
---

> **What this is.** A bullet-point distillation of (1) why we asked whether targeted electrification (TE) could scale on PGL's planned pipeline-replacement footprint, (2) what we found, (3) the structural critique of the current PRP and emerging NPA processes that those findings reveal, and (4) what we want the ICC to order. **This is not a report and not a draft of testimony.** It is an editable input intended to be fed (along with rate-case context and the IL testimony style guide) into a follow-up prompt that will produce a Q&A skeleton of CUB's prefiled expert testimony.

---

## 1. Why we asked the question

### The policy tension

- Chicago has a stated electrification goal: **roughly 1 in 3 residential buildings electrified over the next 10 years**, per the Chicago Climate Action Plan.
- PGL is operating under a state directive that points the opposite direction: in PGL Docket 26-0065, PGL has filed a Pipe Retirement Program ("PRP") that would **retire and replace ~1,020 miles of cast-iron and ductile-iron mains by 2035** — roughly 1 in 4 of the pipes in its system — at ~$214M of project spend in 2026 and ~$306M in 2027.
- The two trajectories are in direct conflict on the same ratepayer base:
  - The City is trying to take residential buildings off gas.
  - The utility is rebuilding the gas system underneath them.
  - The same households are being asked to fund both.
- This isn't just a policy abstraction. It is a real cost question, because every dollar of PRP spend on a block that ends up electrifying anyway is a dollar that ratepayers pay twice.

### The ICC opened the door

- The ICC's PRP Investigation explicitly opened the door to non-pipeline alternatives. As part of that investigation, the ICC convened an **NPA workshop** with two stated motivations:
  1. **Cheaper ways to fix leaks** (lining, repair, smaller-scope retirements, etc.) — straightforward cost reduction.
  2. **Doing leak fixing and targeted electrification together** where it is cost-effective to do so — the harder and more interesting question.
- That second motivation is the one our analysis takes up. The natural follow-up question — and the question CUB / Switchbox set out to answer — is:
  - **Of the residential blocks that PGL is already planning to do PRP work on, how many would be cheaper to electrify than to replace pipe on?**
- This is a **scoping question**, not a policy advocacy question. We are asking: how big is the cost-effective TE opportunity inside a pipeline scope that PGL itself has already defined?

---

## 2. What we did (scope, in one breath)

- **Sample.** We looked at **1,488 fully residential blocks** inside PGL's planned PRP project areas, out of **2,057 total blocks** in those areas. Commercial, industrial, and mixed-use blocks were excluded because their electrification costs are far less well-established.
- **Households in scope.** Those 1,488 blocks contain **28,689 residential units** (16,707 single-family parcels + 11,982 multi-family units).
- **Pipeline mileage in scope.** Of PGL's ~1,451 remaining PRP miles system-wide, ~148 fall inside the mapped planned project areas, of which **~108 miles are on these fully residential blocks.**
- **Cost comparison.** For each block, we compared the **upfront capital cost of pipeline replacement** against the **upfront capital cost of electrifying every unit on the block + decommissioning that block's mains + upgrading the local electric grid**.
- **Cost inputs** (all documented in the report appendix and the open Google Sheet of assumptions):
  - Pipeline replacement: **~$4.96M / mile** (PGL Docket 24-0081).
  - Pipeline decommissioning: **~$66K / mile** (PGL Docket 24-0081).
  - Single-family electrification: **~$29K / unit** (ComEd Whole Home Electrification Program).
  - Multi-family electrification: **~$15K / unit** (ComEd Whole Home Electrification Program).
  - Grid upgrade: **$209.74 / peak kW**, ~$944 per household (E3 VDER in Illinois).
  - Scattershot trajectory: **30% of units electrify over 10 years, 5% discount rate** (Chicago CAP).
- **Conservative on purpose.** The analysis is set up to *under*-estimate, not over-estimate, the case for TE:
  - Capital costs only — no fuel-bill savings, no avoided gas-system O&M, no rate-base / return-on-equity effects, no carbon or health benefits.
  - Average cost-per-mile for replacement; per-unit averages for SF / MF electrification (in reality, the cheapest blocks to electrify and the most expensive to repipe are likely correlated).
  - 100% participation assumed.
  - Current cost estimates only — no learning curve, no policy-driven cost declines.
- The numbers below are therefore most usefully read as a **lower bound on the cost-effective scale of TE**, not as a point estimate.

---

## 3. What we found — three lenses, three answers

We evaluated cost-effectiveness through **three different lenses**, each answering a different question. They are not competing hypotheses; they are progressively less restrictive ways of asking the same comparison. The headline is that **depending on which lens the Commission adopts, the share of these blocks that is cost-effective to electrify is somewhere between a quarter and all of them. There is no defensible reading of the data under which the answer is "approximately none."**

### Lens 1 — Strict block-level (NPA cost ≤ PRP cost, block by block)

- **Question this lens answers.** Where is electrification *immediately, today, no financial engineering* cheaper than pipeline replacement, comparing a single block to itself?
- **Intuition.** This is the most conservative possible test. Every block stands on its own. Cheap blocks do not subsidize expensive ones. If electrifying that block, decommissioning that block's pipe, and upgrading the grid for that block costs less than replacing that block's pipe, the block "passes."
- **Top line: 23.5% of blocks pass — 350 of 1,488.**
  - 3,558 residential units affected (2,449 SF + 1,109 MF).
  - 23.9 miles of pipe replacement avoided.
  - **~$28.2M in net upfront-capital savings** ($90.3M NPA cost vs. $118.5M PRP cost across these 350 blocks).
- **What it means.** Even under the strictest possible cost-effectiveness test, **roughly a quarter of the planned residential PRP scope is already cheaper to electrify than to repipe.** This is the floor. Any cost-effectiveness lens the Commission adopts that is less restrictive than this one will return a larger number.

### Lens 2 — Portfolio cost-neutrality ("banking" savings)

- **Question this lens answers.** If we use the savings from the cheapest-to-electrify blocks to pay for electrification on the next-cheapest blocks, **how far can we get without spending a dollar more than the PRP would have cost?**
- **Intuition.** PGL was already going to spend that money on pipes. Spending the same dollars on the cheapest TE projects first, then "banking" the savings to fund moderately-priced TE projects, is functionally identical from the ratepayer's perspective — same total spend, different infrastructure delivered. This is also explicitly aligned with the ICC's stated objectives: lower leak risk, advance electrification, hold costs steady.
- **Top line: 55.2% of blocks — ~821 blocks** (the point at which the cumulative-savings curve, with blocks ranked cheapest-first by NPA-to-PRP ratio, first crosses zero).
- **What it means.** **Using the same dollars PGL was going to spend on PRP — but spending them on TE in cheapest-first order — gets us to a majority of the residential portfolio at zero net additional cost.** This is not a more expensive program than the PRP. It is the same program with different physical outputs.

### Lens 3 — Portfolio-level with scattershot electrification

- **Question this lens answers.** What is the **real PRP counterfactual**? Some households will electrify on their own over the next decade regardless of what PGL does — driven by gas-bill increases, equipment failures, federal incentives, climate policies, customer preference. So the true cost of "do PRP" is not just the pipe cost; it is the pipe cost **plus** the cost of electrifying that fraction of households *anyway*, on top of pipe that is no longer needed for them.
- **Intuition.** PGL replacing pipe doesn't avoid scattershot electrification. It stacks on top of it. The honest comparison is not "TE vs. PRP" but "coordinated TE vs. PRP + uncoordinated background electrification."
- **Modeling assumption.** 30% of units electrify over 10 years, distributed evenly across years, 5% discount rate (sourced from the Chicago Climate Action Plan trajectory).
- **Top line: across all 1,488 blocks, the total cost of coordinated TE stays *below* the total cost of pipe replacement + scattershot.** At the block level, **60.1% of blocks are individually cheaper to electrify than to repipe-plus-scattershot.**
- **What it means.** Continuing PRP on a contracting customer base is buying two energy systems for the price of two-and-a-half. **Coordinated electrification is the cheap option even before counting any avoided gas-bill, decarbonization, or air-quality benefit.** The portfolio-level cumulative savings under this comparison are *never* negative.

### What predicts whether a block is cost-effective

- **Density is the dominant predictor.** Lower-density single-family blocks and blocks with vacant lots are typically the cheap-to-electrify ones. High-density multi-family blocks are typically the expensive ones.
- **Honest caveat.** This is partly a real-world fact (more pipe per resident on low-density blocks → more cost per resident to repipe) and partly an artifact of how we model cost (avg $/mile × miles vs. avg $/unit × units → density mechanically drives the ratio). We flag this, but density remains useful as a screening signal because density data is publicly available.
- **Geographic implication.** Different PRP project areas — and therefore different Chicago neighborhoods — have very different fractions of cost-effective blocks. Lincoln Square, Garfield Ridge, and Englewood span the spectrum.

### One-line synthesis

- **Depending on the cost-effectiveness lens the Commission adopts, the cost-effective share of the residential PRP footprint is somewhere between ~25% (strict block-level), ~55% (portfolio cost-neutral), and 100% (portfolio with scattershot).** All three lenses are defensible; none of them returns a small number. The dispute is not over whether there is a sizable cost-effective TE opportunity inside the planned PRP. The dispute is over how big it is — and that range is *all* large.

---

## 4. The takeaway: the current PRP process and the emerging NPA evaluation process cannot capture this potential

This is the corollary of the findings, and it is the part of the argument that justifies asking for anything at all in this rate case.

### How the current process works

- **The PRP process, as scoped in the PRP Investigation, is *risk-first*.** Projects are ranked by JANA-style probabilistic risk scoring, and PGL selects projects in approximately riskiest-first order, evaluating one project at a time.
- **The emerging NPA evaluation process, as initially discussed at the ICC's NPA workshop, sits *on top of* that risk-first ordering.** The NPA question — "could we do a non-pipe alternative on this project instead?" — is asked **after** the project has already been selected for its risk score, not before.

### Why that combination cannot capture the cost-effective potential we found

Two structural failure modes — both fatal, both endogenous to the ordering itself, neither solvable by tweaking parameters within the existing framework.

- **Failure mode 1: time-to-block-participation is fatal under risk-first ordering.**
  - Even on a block where TE is unambiguously cheaper, signing up every household on the block — even with strong incentives — takes years. People retire heating equipment on their own schedule, not the utility's.
  - But under a risk-first ordering, the NPA question is being asked about **the riskiest pipe segment of the year**. The answer is structurally: "we cannot wait three years for full block participation; the pipe will fail; replace it now."
  - **Cost-effective TE never wins the timing fight under risk-first ordering**, no matter how cost-effective it is.

- **Failure mode 2: hydraulic feasibility is endogenous to the ordering.**
  - Whether a given pipe segment can actually be decommissioned — pressures balance, no stranded customers downstream — depends critically on **which adjacent segments have already been decommissioned**. Decommissioning is a network problem, not a project-by-project problem.
  - A risk-first ordering is **essentially random** with respect to the network constraint. It selects the segment that scores highest on probabilistic risk, without regard for whether that segment is reachable in the decommissioning sequence.
  - **The realized share of pipe that turns out to be hydraulically decommissionable under risk-first ordering will be far below the share that *could* have been decommissionable under a sequence chosen to maximize feasibility.** This is true mathematically, before any data is collected.
  - This is the missing data the report cannot fill in: we don't know what the maximum decommissionable share is, because nobody — including PGL — has run the sequencing problem.

### The net consequence

- Under the current process and the emerging NPA add-on, **the realized share of the cost-effective TE potential captured will be a small fraction of even the most conservative finding above** — i.e., a small fraction of 23.5%, not a small fraction of the full portfolio.
- The 23.5% / 55.2% / 100% findings describe **what a rational planner could capture in principle.** The current process cannot, by construction, capture more than a small fraction of even the smallest of those numbers.
- This is **not a tuning problem. It is a structural one.** No adjustment to JANA risk weights, NPA decision criteria, or screening thresholds inside the existing framework changes the conclusion. The framework selects against TE in two independent, compounding ways.

### What the right structural fix would look like

- **A parallel "second track."** Not a replacement for the existing process; an addition to it.
  - **Track 1 (existing, possibly slimmer):** Continue risk-first replacement on the truly highest-risk pipe — the segments that genuinely cannot wait. This is a smaller number of projects per year than today.
  - **Track 2 (new):** Plan proactively, in a sequence chosen to **maximize hydraulic decommissioning feasibility**, on the subset of blocks where TE is cost-effective. This track does not look like the current PRP. It is run "from the ends in" rather than "from the riskiest out."
- The two tracks together handle real safety risk **and** capture the cost-effective TE the PRP investigation was already looking for. Neither track alone does both.
- **This is the strategic destination.** It is not what we are asking the Commission to order in *this* case (see §5). It is the regulatory architecture that the ask in §5 is designed to make possible in a future case.

---

## 5. The ask — what we want the ICC to order

The ask is deliberately narrow. CUB is not the lead substantive intervenor on TE in this docket — Illinois PIRG (with Groundwork Data) is doing the heavy lifting on how to actually execute targeted electrification. Our role is to use the report to make a sharp, narrow, technically defensible contribution that **establishes the missing piece of the record**: how much of the PRP scope is actually decommissionable, and on what sequence.

### What we are asking for

A two-part order, each part drafted to be (a) legally defensible (no order to electrify any specific block), (b) incremental (no redirection of capital), and (c) compatible with allied intervenors (no conflict with PIRG / Groundwork Data on TE-mechanism questions).

### Part 1 — Order PGL to conduct a hydraulic-feasibility-and-sequencing study

- **Scope of the study.** The universe of mains currently planned for retirement under the PRP, as filed in PGL's PRP project plan in Docket 26-0065 (~1,020 miles, 179 projects).
- **The study must, at minimum, answer:**
  1. **Sequencing maximum.** For that universe of pipe, what sequence of project execution maximizes the cumulative share of mileage that can be physically decommissioned (not stranded by upstream/downstream pressure or service constraints)?
  2. **Decommissionable upper bound.** Under that maximizing sequence, what is the upper bound on the share of the planned PRP miles — and the share of the planned project areas — that is hydraulically feasible to decommission?
  3. **Cost distribution by project type, not a single average.** What is the actual cost distribution across project types under the optimized sequence? This directly addresses PGL's own observation in the curtain-raise Q&A that "they don't all cost the same." We assumed an average; PGL has the data to do better.
  4. **Capability requirements.** What engineering planning, contractor staging, customer engagement, and procurement capabilities would PGL need to execute a Track 2 program at the cadence implied by the 2035 retirement deadline?
- **Why frame it this way.** This is **the analysis PGL itself would need to know what it can decommission.** The ask is not a novel demand. The ask is for the ICC to require PGL to actually run that analysis, in writing, on the record, where stakeholders can see it.

### Part 2 — Order PGL to release the inputs in a new docketed proceeding

- **Public inputs.** All inputs to the study that *can* be made public must be made public, so other parties can replicate, sanity-check, and extend.
- **Sensitive inputs under NDA.** The truly sensitive inputs — most importantly **PGL's hydraulic / low-pressure system map** and any customer-level data — must be made available to qualified stakeholders under NDA, in a **new proceeding scoped around this question.** PGL's hydraulic map is the single piece of data that no third party can reconstruct independently. Without it, no one — not CUB, not PIRG, not Staff, not the AG — can verify PGL's claims about what is and isn't feasible.
- **Why this matters (state diplomatically).** PGL has the data; intervenors don't. PGL also has a not-unreasonable institutional incentive to characterize hydraulic feasibility as low. Transparency, not adversarial argument, is the antidote. A new proceeding with NDA-protected access lets the disagreement be resolved with shared facts rather than competing assertions.

### What the two-part ask sets up (and why it is the right next step)

- **Step 1 (this docket).** Establish on the record, via the ordered study, what the **upper bound on hydraulically decommissionable PRP miles** actually is.
- **Step 2 (the new proceeding).** Validate or contest that bound under shared (NDA-protected) data with qualified intervenors.
- **Step 3 (a future docket).** With that maximum quantified and on the record, the ICC has the basis to **open a proceeding to reform the PRP / NPA process to incorporate proactive sequencing and TE at scale** — i.e., the Track 2 architecture from §4. This is where the substantive policy change happens.
- **The slogan.** *Getting serious about TE, knowledge first.* You cannot plan for the share of the pipe that's decommissionable if you have not measured it. We are not asking the Commission to commit to a number. We are asking the Commission to require that the number be measured.

---

## 6. Why this is the right ask (defensive bullets)

These are the bullets we'll need to defend the ask in cross or surrebuttal. They are written to be lifted into Q&A.

### Why narrow

- **It does not order TE.** No specific block is required to be electrified. No specific cost is reallocated from PRP to electrification.
- **It does not take a position on the legal mechanism for compelling TE.** That is a separate question and a different intervenor's lane.
- **It does not contest PGL's safety case.** Risk-first replacement on the truly highest-risk pipe continues unaffected.

### Why incremental

- **PGL is being asked to do an analysis and release inputs.** That is well within the scope of what the ICC can order in a rate case under standard prudence-and-reasonableness review.
- **The cost of the study itself is small** relative to the ~$520M of project spend in the 2026–2027 PRP scope.
- **It is consistent with the ICC's own stated direction** in opening the NPA workshop — the Commission has already asked the question this study answers; this ask just operationalizes it.

### Why compatible with the rest of the case

- **Illinois PIRG / Groundwork Data are the lead substantive intervenor on TE mechanisms.** Our contribution sits beside their work, not in front of it. We provide the missing scoping number; they make the case for how to act on it.
- **No friction with allied intervenors.** Nothing in the ask cuts across CUB's traditional consumer-affordability lane, AG's revenue-requirement lane, or the City's climate-and-equity lane.

### Why this resolves an information asymmetry the Commission cannot resolve any other way

- **Only PGL has the hydraulic system map.** No third party can reconstruct it. No analytical workaround substitutes for it.
- **PGL has the institutional incentive to under-report decommissionable feasibility.** Not because PGL is acting in bad faith, but because the framing "this isn't feasible" is an easier defense of the existing capital plan than the framing "this is feasible but here's why we're choosing not to."
- **A docketed proceeding with NDA-protected access converts the disagreement from rhetorical to empirical.** That is exactly the kind of dispute the Commission is best equipped to resolve.

### Why this is justified by the report's findings

- The findings show **between 25% and 100% of the residential PRP footprint is cost-effective to electrify**, depending on the lens.
- The structural critique in §4 shows **the realized share captured under the current process is approximately none**.
- That gap — between cost-effective potential and realized capture — is the value the ask is designed to recover. The first step in recovering it is measuring how much of it is hydraulically reachable.

### What we are *not* claiming (carefully bounded)

- We are **not** claiming a specific number of blocks should be electrified. The data does not yet support a specific number; that's the point of the ask.
- We are **not** claiming our cost numbers are precise. They are conservative averages built from public data, and they are documented in the report appendix. Better numbers — including the actual project-by-project costs PGL has — should replace ours.
- We are **not** claiming hydraulic feasibility is high. We are claiming **nobody currently knows what it is, including PGL, because nobody has done the sequencing analysis.** The whole point of Part 1 of the ask is to find out.

---

## Appendix — pointers for the testimony skeleton

For the prompt that consumes this document tomorrow:

- **The ask** (§5) goes in the recommendation/conclusion section of the testimony.
- **The findings** (§3) anchor the substantive section. Each lens should probably be its own Q&A pair; the synthesis bullet at the end of §3 is the section-closer.
- **The structural critique** (§4) is the bridge between findings and ask. It is the most rhetorically loaded section and likely benefits from a "How do you respond to PGL's claim that the existing PRP and NPA processes are sufficient?" framing.
- **The policy context** (§1) is the section-II "purpose of testimony" framing. The "1 in 3 vs. 1 in 4" tension is the lede.
- **The defensive bullets** (§6) are pre-loaded answers to anticipated cross-examination and should be distributed across the relevant Q&A pairs rather than confined to a single section.
- All numbers in this document trace to the existing analysis in [reports/il_npa/index.qmd](index.qmd) and the notebooks under [reports/il_npa/notebooks/](notebooks/). Nothing here is computed for the first time; this document only re-presents and frames the existing results.
